metadata.title: UniMax: Fairer and More Effective Language Sampling for Large-Scale Multilingual Pretraining

sections.title: Sampling Strategy
content: Standard temperature sampling: $p_l \propto n_l^{1/\tau}$.UniMax sampling: $q_l = \frac{p'_l}{\sum_{l' \in L} p'_{l'}}$ where $p'_l$ is capped.

tables.description: Table showing temperature settings for various multilingual models like mBERT, XLM, and mT5

sections.title: Training Dynamics
content: Figure 2: Pretraining cross-entropy loss on the held-out data over the training steps. With too-low temperature, low-resource languages are sampled too little, and their losses are relatively high. With higher temperature, overfitting becomes more severe with increasing model size.

sections.title: Downstream Performance (TyDi QA)
content: Figure 4: Average TyDi QA GoldP performance across three model sizes. Overall, UNIMAX outperforms both baselines at all model sizes considered. Breakdowns on higher-resource (top-5) and lower-resource (bottom-4) languages show UNIMAX outperforms tau=3.33 on both high- and low-resource, and only underperforms tau=1 on high-resource at large model scales.

sections.title: Long-term Training (1M Steps)
content: Figure 3: Pretraining cross-entropy loss on the held-out data over 1M training steps. With the sequence length of 512, this corresponds to 1/2 character budget. The overfitting behavior emerges only after sufficient number of training steps for tau=3.33.

sections.title: Main Results
content: Table 3: Comparison to mT5. XNLI and PAWS-X show average per-language accuracy; the rest show average per-language EM/F1. We use the translate-train setting except for TyDi QA.

tables.description: Main results table comparing UniMax (umT5) to mT5 across XNLI, PAWS-X, XQuAD, MLQA, and TyDi QA benchmarks

sections.title: Resources
content: umT5 checkpoints, available via T5X repo
